text.title: AI Layout Review Core Rules
text.summary: Ten checks every AI-generated mockup must pass before it ships to engineering or enters the design system. Each carries a hard verdict (block / request-changes / approve / approve-with-note).

rule.statement: Mockup must cite at least one named user-research artifact (interview transcript, usability test id, support-ticket cluster, JTBD job statement). The artifact must be linked or referenced in the PR/Figma comment. No artifact → BLOCK.

rationale.source: Faion gap analysis pain explicitly named; Nielsen Norman 'AI UI without research' study (2024)

rule.rationale: AI generates layouts by pattern-matching training data, not by reasoning about your users' jobs. The most common pain point named by designers in 2025 is "AI hands me a mock with no grounding, then leadership wants to ship it." A cited research artifact lets the designer prove the mock either matches user evidence or contradicts it.

rule.statement: Every color, font-size, spacing value, radius, and elevation in the mock either maps to a named token in the design system OR is documented as an intentional new token with a token-introduction record. Off-token values without record → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: Design Tokens W3C community spec; Lyft / Shopify token-governance writeups

rule.rationale: AI tools (v0, Galileo) emit raw hex colors and arbitrary spacing that drift from the system. Once one screen ships off-token, the system fractures because subsequent screens copy the new values. Token enforcement is the only thing that keeps a system coherent at scale.

example.title: Off-token drift
example.code: v0-generated card uses #1A73E8 for the primary CTA. Design system primary token is #1E66F5. No new-token record exists. Visually almost identical, semantically different. Verdict: REQUEST_CHANGES — swap to token or open a new-token RFC.

rule.statement: Run WCAG 2.2 AA checks: text contrast ≥ 4.5:1 (≥ 3:1 for large text), UI component contrast ≥ 3:1, focus indicators present and ≥ 3:1 against adjacent colors, target sizes ≥ 24×24 CSS px (AA) or 44×44 (AAA / mobile primary). Any failure → BLOCK.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.2 normative requirements; EU Accessibility Act enforceable from June 2025

rule.rationale: AI image-generated mockups frequently miss focus states entirely (they're not in the training distribution of static screenshots). For EU-facing products, AA is now a legal floor — shipping non-compliant layouts is a regulatory risk, not just a UX one.

rule.statement: For every screen, mockup must show: empty state, loading state, error state, and one realistic-data state with long names, long numbers, and right-to-left script if applicable. Missing states → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: Edge-case-driven design (Smashing Magazine 2024); Shopify Polaris UX patterns

rule.rationale: AI generates the "happy path with hero name 'Jane Doe' and a single $12.99 line item." Real users have 5-line addresses, 47-character company names, and zero-result states. Without edge-state coverage, engineering ships broken UIs that the designer has to fix in production.

rule.statement: Any animation or transition in the mock specifies (a) duration (ms), (b) easing curve, (c) prefers-reduced-motion fallback. Animations without all three → REQUEST_CHANGES. Default to ≤ 200ms for affordance animations, ≤ 400ms for hero animations.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions; CSS prefers-reduced-motion spec; vestibular-disorder UX guidance

rule.rationale: AI tools generate animations from prompts like "make it slide in" without considering vestibular-disorder users or system-level reduced-motion preferences. Shipping without a fallback regresses on accessibility and triggers motion-sickness for ~35% of users with vestibular sensitivity.

rule.statement: UI copy is reviewed for: brand voice match, no placeholder lorem-ipsum-style filler, no AI-generated "marketing-confident" copy ("Revolutionize your workflow", "Powered by intelligent AI"), no hallucinated feature names that don't exist in the product. Failures → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: Mailchimp content style guide; SaaS landing-page copy audits 2024

rule.rationale: AI mocks often invent feature names ("Smart Insights", "Magic Compose") that the product doesn't have. Engineering then builds the layout, marketing scrambles to explain a feature that doesn't exist. Copy hygiene at design time prevents this.

rule.statement: New screen must fit into existing information architecture: navigation depth, breadcrumbs, back-button behavior, in-app linking targets. Screen that contradicts current IA without an IA-RFC → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: Information Architecture: For the Web and Beyond (Rosenfeld et al.); production app navigation regression patterns

rule.rationale: AI generates a screen in isolation; it doesn't know your nav model. A "new screen" without IA grounding causes orphan screens, broken back-buttons, and broken deep links — the kind of paper-cut bug that erodes trust over months.

rule.statement: Mockup matches the brand guideline on: typography hierarchy, illustration vs photography rule, button shape language, signature components (header, footer, CTA card). Cross-brand drift (a B2B product suddenly looking like Linear because v0's default is "Linear-ish") → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: Brand-system audits; AI design tool default-style analysis (Awwwards 2025)

rule.rationale: v0 / Galileo / Figma Make have visual defaults (rounded corners, neutral grays, sans-serif). Mocks created from open prompts inherit these defaults and erode brand differentiation. The most common 2025 brand-decay vector is "we shipped a v0 page and it looks like everyone else's v0 page."

rule.statement: Mockup uses existing design-system components by name (Button/primary, Card/elevated, Input/text) rather than recreated free-form rectangles. Free-form recreations of existing components → REQUEST_CHANGES with link to the component to use.

rationale.source: Figma component-instance best practices; design-system ROI research (Sparkbox 2024)

rule.rationale: AI tools recreate components from scratch because they don't have your Figma library context. Engineering then asks "is this the existing Button or a new variant?" — the question that kills design-system velocity. Component reuse must be enforced at review.

rule.statement: Reviewer records: which AI tool generated the mock, prompt or input image used, approximate % of the artifact that is AI-generated vs human-edited, and the link to the source asset. Missing attestation → REQUEST_CHANGES.

rationale.source: EU AI Act transparency requirements; design-system provenance audits

rule.rationale: Future debugging ("why does this screen contradict the research?"), regulatory audits, and design-system governance need to know which artifacts started as AI output. Capturing the metadata at review time is far cheaper than reconstructing it from Figma history.
